title: What Buyers See When They Value a Building Business
linkedin:
A building business with $600,000 in normalised profit. A buyer's offer of $1.4 million. The gap between expectation and offer is almost never about the quality of the work.

It is almost always about three construction-specific mechanics that compress what a buyer sees in the accounts.

The first is work in progress. WIP revenue recognition is technically correct accounting. A pattern of WIP write-downs shortly after financial year end is not. It signals that reported earnings were optimistic. Buyers discount normalised EBITDA based on the credibility of the WIP schedule, even when the business is genuinely profitable.

The second is retention balances. Two and a half to five percent of every contract sits on the balance sheet as a receivable. A buyer treats it as impaired. It is not cash, takes twelve to twenty-four months to collect across a portfolio, and is subject to disputes not always visible in the P&L.

The third is owner dependence. In Queensland, the QBCC builder's licence is held personally by the nominee, not the company. Client relationships built over a decade are tied to the principal. Both factors typically require an earn-out arrangement, which changes the effective sale price regardless of the headline multiple.

Construction and trades businesses in Australia typically trade at two to four times normalised EBITDA. These three factors determine where in that range any specific business lands.

EOFY, with a full year of project data available, is the natural moment to understand where the current business sits and what is driving the number.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

Most building business owners have a rough sense of what their business might be worth. But when a buyer opens the books, three things consistently land the offer well below expectations: WIP schedule credibility, retention receivables treated as impaired, and a builder's licence that cannot transfer with the company.

What has your EOFY data shown you about how a buyer would read your accounts?

Our latest post covers exactly what buyers look for when they value a building or trades business in Australia.

google:
For building and trades business owners across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, the weeks after June 30 offer more than just tax reporting.

A full year of project data is available. The WIP schedule reflects a complete picture. Retention balances span twelve months of contracts. For any owner who has thought about what the business might eventually be worth, this is the clearest view of the year.

What surprises most building business owners who go through a formal valuation is how significantly construction-specific mechanics shape the number a buyer arrives at. Work in progress accounting, retention receivables, and the personal nature of the QBCC builder's licence all affect how a buyer reads the accounts. These factors often compress the apparent earnings before any negotiation begins.

Construction and trades businesses in Australia typically trade at two to four times normalised EBITDA. Where in that range a specific business lands depends on these structural factors more than on revenue size.

Our latest ProfitPulse post covers the three mechanics that most consistently affect building business valuations: what buyers look for in WIP documentation, why retention balances are treated differently from other receivables, and how owner dependence affects both price and deal structure.

If you are a building or trades business owner thinking about what the current business is actually worth, the EOFY accounts are the right place to start.

Read the full post: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-buyers-see-when-they-value-a-building-business
